q: An ideal gas occupies a volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>6</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi><mi>a</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The energy of the gas is:

solution:
V=2 m3

P=3×106 Pa

PV=nRT

E=f2nRT=f2PV=32×3×106×2

=9×106J

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of an ideal gas is increased from 200 K to 800 K. If r.m.s. speed of gas at 200 K is v0. Then, r.m.s. speed of the gas at 800 K will be:
question: The temperature of an ideal gas is increased from 200 K to 800 K. If r.m.s. speed of gas at 200 K is v0. Then, r.m.s. speed of the gas at 800 K will be:
solution:
At 200 K, the rms speed is given by

v0=3R×200M   ...(1)

At 800 K, the rms speed is given by

v'=3R×800M   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) and solve to calculate the required speed.

v'v0=3R×800M3R×200M=800200= 2⇒v'=2v0

subject: Physics
q:
In the isothermal expansion of $10 \mathrm{~g}$ of gas from volume $\mathrm{V}$ to $2 \mathrm{~V}$ the work done by the gas is $575 \mathrm{~J}$. 
What is the root mean square speed of the molecules of the gas at that temperature?
question:
In the isothermal expansion of $10 \mathrm{~g}$ of gas from volume $\mathrm{V}$ to $2 \mathrm{~V}$ the work done by the gas is $575 \mathrm{~J}$. 
What is the root mean square speed of the molecules of the gas at that temperature?
solution: $\mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{rms}}=\sqrt{\frac{3 \rho \mathrm{v}}{\text { mass of the gas }}}$

subject: Physics
q: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
question: A gas mixture consists of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> moles of argon and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> moles of oxygen at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math>. Neglecting all vibrational modes, the total internal energy of the system is
solution:
As we know, internal energy is given by, U=nCvT. Also argon gas is monoatomic and oxygen gas is diatomic.

Therefore, total internal energy will beUnet=n1CV1T+n2CV2T

=8×3R2×T+6×5R2×T

=27RT

subject: Physics
q:
Same gas is filled in two vessels of the same volume at the same temperature. If the ratio of the number of molecules is 1:4, then

A. The r.m.s. velocity of gas molecules in two vessels will be the same.

B. The ratio of pressure in these vessels will be 1:4.

C. The ratio of pressure will be 1:1.

D. The r.m.s. velocity of gas molecules in two vessels will be in the ratio of 1:4.
question:
Same gas is filled in two vessels of the same volume at the same temperature. If the ratio of the number of molecules is 1:4, then

A. The r.m.s. velocity of gas molecules in two vessels will be the same.

B. The ratio of pressure in these vessels will be 1:4.

C. The ratio of pressure will be 1:1.

D. The r.m.s. velocity of gas molecules in two vessels will be in the ratio of 1:4.
solution:
The rms velocity of a gas is given by Vrms=3RTM. Therefore,Vrms is independent of number of moles, Hence, statement A is correct and D is incorrect.

According to the ideal gas equation PV=nRT⇒P∝n

⇒P1P2=n1 n2=14

Hence, statement B is correct and C is incorrect.

Ans [A & B only are correct]

subject: Physics
q: A monoatomic gas of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi></math> is kept in an insulated container. The container is moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the container is suddenly stopped then a change in temperature of the gas (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math>gas constant) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is,
question: A monoatomic gas of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi></math> is kept in an insulated container. The container is moving with velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the container is suddenly stopped then a change in temperature of the gas (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math>gas constant) is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. Value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is,
solution:
Given that mass of gas is 4u hence its molar mass M is 4 g mol-1

∴12mv2=nCvΔT

⇒12 m×302=mM×3R2×ΔT

∴  ΔT=36003R

q: Using equipartition of energy, the specific heat (in J kg-1 K-1 ) of Aluminium at high temperature can be estimated to be (atomic weight of Aluminium =27)
question: Using equipartition of energy, the specific heat (in J kg-1 K-1 ) of Aluminium at high temperature can be estimated to be (atomic weight of Aluminium =27)
solution:
Solid atom possesses 6 degrees of freedom due to vibrations at high temperature.

Cv=12fR=6R2=3R for 1 mole
∴ Specific heat capacity in J kg-1 K-1=CvM

=3RM=3×8.31427×1000

≈925

question: The root-mean-square speed of molecules of a given mass of a gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> atmosphere pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The root-mean-square speed of molecules of the gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>127</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> atmosphere pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> will be __________.
solution:
vrms=3RTM⇒vrms∝T⇒vrms2vrms1=T2 T1=400300=23

vrms2=23vrms1=23×200⇒vrms2=4003 m s-1.

The ratio of their respective mean free times is closest to:
question: Two gases - argon (atomic radius <math><mn>0.07</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> atomic weight <math><mn>40</mn></math> ) and xenon (atomic radius <math><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>,</mo></math> atomic weight <math><mn>140</mn></math> ) have the same number density and are at the same temperature. tArtXe=dXe2dAr2×m1m2
=0.10.072×40140
=1072×27=1.09

q: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
question: What will be the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi><mo> </mo><mo>?</mo></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></math> is Boltzmann constant)
solution:
Energy associated with each degree of freedom per molecule =12kBT.

Hence, the average value of energy along one degree of freedom for an ideal gas in thermal equilibrium at a temperature T =12kBT.

subject: Physics
q: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature. The ratio $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}} / \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$ for the gas is
question: During an adiabatic process, the pressure of a gas is found to be proportional to the cube of its absolute temperature. The ratio $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}} / \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}$ for the gas is
solution:
$\mathrm{P} \propto \mathrm{T}^3 \quad\left(\frac{\mathrm{P}_1}{\mathrm{P}_2}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{T}_1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}\right)^3$
Comparing it with standard eq. $\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}}=\gamma=\frac{3}{2}$

subject: Physics
q:
A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen has volume 2000 cm3, temperature 300 K, pressure 100kPa and mass 0.76 g. The ratio of number of moles of hydrogen to number of moles of oxygen in the mixture will be

[Take gas constant R=8.3 J K-1 mol-1]
question:
A mixture of hydrogen and oxygen has volume 2000 cm3, temperature 300 K, pressure 100kPa and mass 0.76 g. The ratio of number of moles of hydrogen to number of moles of oxygen in the mixture will be

[Take gas constant R=8.3 J K-1 mol-1]
solution:
From the ideal gas equation, total number of moles in the mixture are,

n=PVRT=2×10-3×100×103×325×300⇒n=225=0.08

If number of moles of oxygen are n1 and of hydrogen are n2

n1+n2=n=0.08…1

And total mass of the mixture

n132+n22=0.76…2

Solving the above equations,

n1=0.02 and n2=0.06

n2n1=3

subject: Physics
q: An ideal gas undergoes a quasi-static, reversible process in which its molar heat capacity C remains constant. If during this process the relation of pressure P and volume V is given by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant, then n is given by (Here <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> are molar specific heat at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively) :
question: An ideal gas undergoes a quasi-static, reversible process in which its molar heat capacity C remains constant. If during this process the relation of pressure P and volume V is given by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo></math> constant, then n is given by (Here <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></mrow></msub></math> are molar specific heat at constant pressure and constant volume, respectively) :
solution:
PVn=k

C=Cv+R1-n ; C-Cv=R1-n

1-n=RC-Cv ; n=1-RC-Cv

n=C-Cv-RC-Cv ; n=C-Cv-Cp-CvC-Cv

n=C-Cv-Cp+CvC-Cv ; n=C-CpC-Cv

q: The root mean square speed of molecules of nitrogen gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is approximately: (Given mass of a nitrogen molecule <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and take Boltzmann constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
question: The root mean square speed of molecules of nitrogen gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is approximately: (Given mass of a nitrogen molecule <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>26</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and take Boltzmann constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>k</mi><mi>B</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> )
solution:
The RMS value of a gas is given by 

vrms=3RTM=3kBTm

Substituting the given values,

vrms=3×1.4×10-23×3004.6×10-26
≈523 m s-1

subject: Physics
q:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
question:
The rms speed of oxygen molecule in a vessel at particular temperature is 1+5x12v, when v is the average speed of the molecule. The value of x will be:

(take π=227)
solution:
The RMS speed is given by 

vrms=3RTM

The average speed is given by 

vavg=8RTπM

The ratio is 

⇒vrmsvavg =3RTM8RTπM=3π8=38×227⇒vrmsvavg =3328

Comparing it with the given value 1+5x12,

vrmsvavg =1+528⇒x=28

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of a polyatomic gas<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> must be mixed with two moles of a monoatomic gas so that the mixture behaves as a diatomic gas. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> moles of a polyatomic gas<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>f</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>)</mo></math> must be mixed with two moles of a monoatomic gas so that the mixture behaves as a diatomic gas. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>N</mi></math> is:
solution:
The equivalent degree of freedom feq of the mixture is

feq=n1f1+n2f2n1+n2

For diatomic gas feq=5.

⇒5=(n)(6)+(2)(3)n+2

⇒5n+10=6n+6

⇒n=4

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of a monoatomic gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The density of the gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the order of energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of a monoatomic gas is at a pressure of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The density of the gas is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kg</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. What is the order of energy of the gas due to its thermal motion?
solution:
Internal energy is the energy due to thermal motion.

The internal energy of an ideal gas given by

U=f2nRT (for n moles)

U=f2PV=f2×P×Mρ (Since, f=3  for a monoatomic gas.)

U=32×4×104×28

=1.5×104J
So, the order of energy due to thermal motion is 104

subject: Physics
q: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
question: Nitrogen gas is at 300 °C temperature. The temperature (in K ) at which the rms speed of a H2 molecule would be equal to the rms speed of a nitrogen molecule, is ………. (Molar mass of N2 gas 28 g ).
solution:
Given,

Temperature of Nitrogen gas is 

T1=300 °C=300+273=573 K

Molar mass of H2 gas is MH2=2 g

Molar mass of N2 gas is MN2=28 g

The rms speed of gas is given by

 Vrms=3RTM

Now rms speed of hydrogen gas is equal to rms speed of nitrogen gas, i.e.; VrmsH2=VrmsN2
⇒TH2TN2=MH2MN2

Substitute the given values i above equation, we get,

TH2=228×573

TH2=41 K

subject: Physics
q: A flask contains hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of hydrogen and oxygen respectively is :
question: A flask contains hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn></math> by mass at temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The ratio of average kinetic energy per molecule of hydrogen and oxygen respectively is :
solution:
Average Kinetic Energy per molecule KEavg=32kBT. Therefore, we can conclude that the average kinetic energy of the molecules is directly proportional to the temperature of the gas and is independent of pressure, volume or the nature of the gas.

Hence, required ratio =1:1

question: 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=7 / 5$ is mixed with 1 mole of a gas with $\gamma=5 / 3$, then the value of $\gamma$ for the resulting mixture is
solution: $\gamma$ for resulting mixture should be in between $7 / 5$ and $5 / 3$

q: The correct relation between the degrees of freedom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> and the ratio of specific heat <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> is:
question: The correct relation between the degrees of freedom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> and the ratio of specific heat <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>γ</mi></math> is:

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: If the average kinetic energy of $H_2$ and $O_2$ molecules, kept in two different sized containers are same, then their temperatures will be same.

Reason R: The r.m.s. speed of $H_2$ and $O_2$ molecules are same at same temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: If the average kinetic energy of $H_2$ and $O_2$ molecules, kept in two different sized containers are same, then their temperatures will be same.

Reason R: The r.m.s. speed of $H_2$ and $O_2$ molecules are same at same temperature.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
The average kinetic energy of a gas molecule is directly proportional to its absolute temperature and is given by $E = \dfrac{f}{2} k_B T$, where $f$ is the degree of freedom. Since both $H_2$ and $O_2$ are diatomic gases, they have the same degrees of freedom ($f=5$). Therefore, if their average kinetic energies are the same, their temperatures must be the same. Thus, Assertion A is true.

The root mean square (r.m.s.) speed of a gas molecule is given by $v_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3RT}{M}}$. Since the molar mass $M$ of $H_2$ ($2 \text{ g/mol}$) is different from that of $O_2$ ($32 \text{ g/mol}$), their r.m.s. speeds will be different even at the same temperature. Thus, Reason R is false.

Therefore, A is true but R is false.

Answer: A is true but R is false

subject: Physics
q: If the r.m.s speed of chlorine molecule is 490 m s-1 at 27oC, the r.m.s speed of argon molecules at the same temperature will be (Atomic mass of argon =39.9 u, molecular mass of chlorine =70.9 u)
question: If the r.m.s speed of chlorine molecule is 490 m s-1 at 27oC, the r.m.s speed of argon molecules at the same temperature will be (Atomic mass of argon =39.9 u, molecular mass of chlorine =70.9 u)
solution:
The formula to calculate the rms speed of the argon molecules is given by

vAr=3RTMAr   ...1

The formula to calculate the rms speed of chlorine molecule is given by

vCl=3RTMCl   ...2

Divide equation (1) by equation (2) and simplify to obtain the required ratio.

vArvCl=MClMAr   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) and solve to calculate the required value.

vAr490 m s-1=7140⇒vAr= 7140×490 m s-1≈651.7 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: If $2$ mole of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$, is mixed with $6$ mole of another ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $2T$ then the temperature of mixture is :
question: If $2$ mole of an ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $T$, is mixed with $6$ mole of another ideal monoatomic gas at temperature $2T$ then the temperature of mixture is :
solution:
Using the principle of conservation of internal energy for the mixture of ideal gases:

$U_{mix} = U_1 + U_2$

$(n_1 + n_2) C_{V} T_{mix} = n_1 C_{V} T_1 + n_2 C_{V} T_2$

Since both gases are monoatomic, their molar heat capacities at constant volume are equal ($C_{V} = \dfrac{3}{2}R$).

$(n_1 + n_2) T_{mix} = n_1 T_1 + n_2 T_2$

Substituting the given values $n_1 = 2$, $T_1 = T$, $n_2 = 6$, and $T_2 = 2T$:

$(2 + 6) T_{mix} = 2(T) + 6(2T)$

$8 T_{mix} = 2T + 12T$

$8 T_{mix} = 14T$

$T_{mix} = \dfrac{14}{8}T = \dfrac{7}{4}T$

Answer: $\dfrac{7}{4}T$

subject: Physics
q: One mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with three moles of a diatomic gas. The molecular specific heat of mixture at constant volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>; then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ . (Assume that the given diatomic gas has no vibrational mode.)
question: One mole of a monoatomic gas is mixed with three moles of a diatomic gas. The molecular specific heat of mixture at constant volume is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>; then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be _____ . (Assume that the given diatomic gas has no vibrational mode.)
solution:
Molecular specific heat at constant volume in terms of degrees of freedom is given by CV=f2R

Molecular specific heat of mixture at constant volume is CV/mix=n1CV1+n2CV2n1+n2

=1×3R2+3×5R21+3

=9R4=α24R

⇒α=3

q: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
question: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
solution:
Let the inter molecular distance be D
Then in a volume of 4 3 π D 3 there is only one molecule.

4 3 π D 3 P=( 1 N A )RT

D= ( 3RT 4π N A P ) 1 3

Substituting P=4× 10 −10 Pa,  R=8.3,   N A =6× 10 23 ,  T=300K

D=0.2 mm

subject: Physics
q:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: The internal energy of a perfect gas is entirely kinetic and depends only on absolute temperature of the gas and not on its pressure or volume.
Statement 2: A perfect gas is heated keeping pressure constant and later at constant volume. For the same amount of heat the temperature of the gas at constant pressure is lower than that at constant volume.
question:
This question has Statement-1 and Statement-2. Of the four choices given after the Statements, choose the one that best describes the two Statements.
Statement 1: The internal energy of a perfect gas is entirely kinetic and depends only on absolute temperature of the gas and not on its pressure or volume.
Statement 2: A perfect gas is heated keeping pressure constant and later at constant volume. For the same amount of heat the temperature of the gas at constant pressure is lower than that at constant volume.
options: Statement-1 is true, Statement- 2 is true and Statement $-2$ is the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is false.
Statement-1 is true, Statement-2 is true but Statement-2 is not the correct explanation of Statement-1.
Statement-1 is false, Statement-2 is true.
solution:
Internal energy of a system is the energy possessed by the system due to molecular motion and molecular configuration. Internal energy $u=u_k+u_p$ For an ideal gas there is no molecular attraction $\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{p}}=0$
$\therefore$ Internal energy $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}$ (i.e., internal kinetic energy)
and $\mathrm{u}=\mathrm{u}_{\mathrm{k}}=\frac{3}{2} \mu \mathrm{RT}$
At constant pressure $(\Delta \mathrm{Q})_{\mathrm{P}}=\mu \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{p}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
At constant volume $(\Delta \mathrm{Q})_{\mathrm{v}}=\mu \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}} \Delta \mathrm{T}$
As C $_{\mathrm{P}}>\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}} \quad \therefore \quad(\Delta \mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{P}} < (\Delta \mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{V}}$

q: A diatomic gas, having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is heated at constant pressure. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>U</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>W</mi></math>
question: A diatomic gas, having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>P</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>7</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>C</mi><mi>V</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>R</mi><mo>,</mo></math> is heated at constant pressure. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>U</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>Q</mi><mo>:</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi>W</mi></math>
solution:
dU=nCvdT⇒dQ=nCpdT⇒dW=PdV=nRdT (isobaric process)

dU:dQ:dW:Cv:Cp:R=5R2:7R2:R=5:7:2.

subject: Physics
q: What will be the effect on the root mean square velocity of oxygen molecules if the temperature is doubled and oxygen molecule dissociates into atomic oxygen?
question: What will be the effect on the root mean square velocity of oxygen molecules if the temperature is doubled and oxygen molecule dissociates into atomic oxygen?
options: The velocity of atomic oxygen remains same
The velocity of atomic oxygen doubles
The velocity of atomic oxygen becomes half
The velocity of atomic oxygen becomes four times
solution:
The RMS velocity of a gas is given by, vRMS=3RTM

When O2 dissociates into O its molecular mass becomes M2. Therefore,

Ratio of RMS speed, v1v2=T1T2×M2M1⇒v1v2=T2T×M/2M⇒v2v1=2
